title: 289-Million-Year-Old Mummy Reptile Reveals the Origins of How We Breathe
summary:
Every breath you take is part of a story that began nearly 300 million years ago. Now, a spectacularly preserved fossil from an Oklahoma cave has revealed the earliest known chapter of that story.

The fossil belongs to Captorhinus aguti, a small, lizard-like reptile that lived during the early Permian period, about 289 million years ago. It was discovered in cave systems near Richards Spur, Oklahoma—a site famous for its extraordinary preservation of ancient life. Oil seeps and oxygen-free mud protected not just the bones, but also delicate soft tissues like skin and cartilage. The result is a three-dimensional "mummy" fossil, frozen in its final resting position with one arm tucked beneath its body.

Using neutron computed tomography (nCT) at a facility in Australia, researchers scanned the fossil without damaging it. What they saw amazed them. "I started to see all these structures wrapped around the bones," said Ethan Mooney, who co-led the study. "They were very thin and textured. And lo and behold, there was a nice wrapping of skin around the torso of this animal." The skin had an accordion-like texture, similar to scales on modern worm lizards.

But the real breakthrough came when the team reconstructed the reptile's breathing system. By studying three Captorhinus specimens, they identified a segmented cartilaginous sternum, sternal ribs, and connections linking the ribcage to the shoulder girdle. This provided direct evidence of costal aspiration breathing—where muscles between the ribs expand and compress the chest cavity to pull air into the lungs. Before this system evolved, early land animals relied on breathing through their skin and using mouth movements to push air into their lungs—a method that limits activity.

"It was a game changer that allowed these animals to adopt a much more active lifestyle," said Mooney, now a PhD candidate at Harvard.

The discovery also included a stunning bonus: using synchrotron infrared spectroscopy, researchers detected traces of original proteins preserved in the fossil's bone, cartilage, and skin. These molecules are the oldest of their kind ever identified, dating back nearly 100 million years earlier than previous examples found in dinosaur fossils. It's a reminder that even the smallest fossils can hold the biggest secrets.
why_it_matters: This fossil provides the earliest direct evidence for the rib-based breathing system that all mammals and reptiles (including humans) use today, showing that a key evolutionary innovation for land animals happened much earlier than previously thought.

keywords.explanation: A small, lizard-like reptile from the early Permian period, preserved as a mummy fossil.
The geological time period around 289 million years ago when this reptile lived.
A scanning technique using neutrons to see inside a fossil without damaging it.
Rib-based breathing where muscles expand and compress the chest to move air in and out of the lungs.
A technique used to detect chemical traces, such as proteins, in the fossil.
Organic molecules found in bone, cartilage, and skin; the oldest ever detected in a fossil.

questions.question: What technique was used to scan the fossil without damaging it?
Which breathing system did the researchers identify in Captorhinus?
What evidence of soft tissues was preserved?
How do the proteins found in this fossil compare to previous finds?
options: they are about 100 million years older than any before
they are younger than dinosaur proteins
they are the same age as dinosaur proteins
they are the youngest ever found
questions.correct_answer: they are about 100 million years older than any before

questions.question: What was the key anatomical feature that indicated costal aspiration?
options: segmented cartilaginous sternum and sternal ribs
questions.correct_answer: segmented cartilaginous sternum and sternal ribs

background_read: The early Permian period (around 299–270 million years ago) was a time when the supercontinent Pangaea was forming. The climate was generally warm and dry, and the first reptiles were diversifying into many niches.

Richards Spur in Oklahoma is one of the most productive fossil sites for early Permian terrestrial vertebrates. It is a cave system where oil seeps and anoxic mud created exceptional preservation conditions, often retaining soft tissues.

Neutron computed tomography differs from X-ray CT by being more sensitive to light elements like hydrogen, making it ideal for imaging soft tissues in fossils. Synchrotron infrared spectroscopy can detect the chemical signatures of original organic molecules such as collagen.

Before costal aspiration, early tetrapods used buccal pumping (mouth movements) and cutaneous respiration (skin breathing), which limited oxygen intake and activity levels. The evolution of costal aspiration was a key innovation for more active terrestrial lifestyles.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Oldest evidence of how we breathe found in a 289-million-year-old mummy fossil
└─ hook: 'Every breath you take is part of a story that began nearly 300 million years ago'
DISCOVERY: Fossil of Captorhinus aguti from Richards Spur, Oklahoma
└─ PRESERVATION: 3D mummy with skin, cartilage, and proteins intact
SCANNING METHOD: Neutron CT at an Australian facility
├─ Observation: 'structures wrapped around the bones' – skin with accordion texture
└─ Quote: Ethan Mooney on seeing soft tissues
BREAKTHROUGH: Reconstruction of costal aspiration breathing
├─ Evidence: Sterum, sternal ribs, connections to shoulder girdle
└─ Before: skin breathing and mouth pumping (less efficient)
BONUS: Detection of original proteins using synchrotron spectroscopy
└─ Significance: oldest proteins ever identified, ~100 Ma older than previous record
└─ Quote: Mooney 'game changer… more active lifestyle'
OPEN QUESTION: How widespread was this breathing system among early reptiles? (implied)

perspectives.description: The combination of exceptional preservation and advanced scanning has revealed the oldest known respiratory system, filling a major gap in the evolution of terrestrial vertebrates.
Interpreting soft tissues from a single specimen is difficult; the presence of costal aspiration relies on correlations with modern animals and could be debated until more fossils are found.
The unusual preservation at Richards Spur suggests that many more soft-tissue fossils may exist there. However, such preservation is extremely rare globally, limiting the sample size.

Now that proteins have been detected, researchers may attempt to sequence them for evolutionary insights. Future scans of other Captorhinus specimens could confirm the breathing system and reveal individual variation.
